input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Digital Twin of a building within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain. The twin merges a static BIM representation of the building’s geometry and component hierarchy with real‑time data streams from embedded sensors, actuators and controllers, enabling continuous simulation, analysis and optimization throughout the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides continuous monitoring through real‑time data exchange, predictive analytics for condition‑based maintenance, operational optimization of building systems, direct control of HVAC, lighting and other building functions via configurable logic, and immersive interactive visualization for inspection and decision support. Target users include Building Control Engineers (defining control logic), Facility Managers (monitoring and operating the building), and DT Engineers (extending functions with custom services).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implemented via a model‑driven deployment framework that enriches BIM models with device and control information, applies Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) techniques to generate deployable twin artifacts, and orchestrates real‑time data flow between the physical asset and the twin. The architecture is modular, exposing standardized extension points for custom services and plugins, and incorporates real‑time data exchange mechanisms and interactive visualization components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework guarantees real‑time, continuous bi‑directional data exchange that synchronises the physical building and its Digital Twin at runtime.  Synchronisation is event‑driven and is designed to minimise latency to the point of real‑time operation, as described in the real‑time data exchange capabilities highlighted in the document.  The architecture supports continuous monitoring and control with explicit emphasis on maintaining low temporal latency and fine‑grained granularity, enabling instant reflection of sensor data and command execution across the twin without full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is orchestrated through a modular, component‑centric architecture that centres on a DT Framework acting as a central hub.  This hub exposes standardized extension points, enabling the seamless integration of custom services, analytics modules, and monitoring interfaces.  The design follows a pattern of loose coupling and runtime configuration management, allowing components to be added, re‑configured or removed on demand without full system redeployment.  Resource management is achieved through dynamic allocation of services and data pipelines, while system‑wide coordination is handled by the hub which routes bidirectional data streams between BIM models, sensor networks and user interfaces, thereby sustaining a holistic life‑cycle management flow.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework is designed to be interoperable across heterogeneous systems, with interoperability highlighted as a core non‑functional requirement. While specific protocols (e.g., REST, MQTT) are not mentioned, the architecture supports seamless integration with external devices, services, and enterprise systems, allowing the Digital Twin to connect to varied infrastructure components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Using Building Information Modeling (Revit/IFC) as the geometric foundation, the framework enriches BIM with device metadata so that sensors, actuators, and controllers are explicitly defined in the model. A Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline extracts these semantic annotations and validates them in a dedicated validation component. The validated data is then converted by a conversion component into runtime twin artifacts, which are instantiated in Eclipse Ditto as a collection of DittoDevice entities. Telemetry and command flows are routed through Eclipse Hono, while the twin’s visual representation is managed in Unreal Engine 5 via TwinComponent instances. Behavioral logic is driven by FeatureParser modules that interpret device metadata (e.g., photometric values for lights) and translate sensor readings or operator actions into actuator commands. The resulting system maintains explicit relationships among the geometric BIM representation, the behavioral twin modules, and the underlying data streams, enabling continuous synchronization and interaction between the physical building and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation devices are captured in the BIM model and represented as DittoDevice entities within the twin. The LightTwinComponent exposes a physical LED light as a controllable element; its API supports commands for on/off, brightness level, and color‑temperature in Kelvin. The UnrealTwinController brokers these commands through the DittoController, which communicates with the physical device via the DittoConnection. Pre‑execution checks validate actuator commands against flow‑and‑risk policies, preventing unsafe or conflicting operations. Although the framework currently implements only the light actuator, additional actuators (e.g., HVAC components referenced by HVACFeatureParser in the documentation) can be incorporated following the same ontology and control pattern.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are embedded within the BIM model through device metadata, enabling each physical sensor to be represented as a DittoDevice in the twin. The framework forwards raw telemetry from the physical sensors to Eclipse Hono for transport to the Ditto platform, where policy‑based validation ensures correct units and value ranges before the data is stored and propagated to the Unreal Engine 5 visualization. Real‑time data exchange guarantees that sensor readings are reflected in the twin continuously. While the specific sensor types and sampling rates are determined by the building’s instrumentation, the architecture supports any time‑continuous or event‑driven sensor streams.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained through a traceable validation pipeline: BIM models are checked against semantic constraints, whereas runtime data undergoes policy‑based validation in Eclipse Ditto to enforce value ranges and unit consistency. Pre‑execution checks on actuator commands further safeguard operational safety. Fault tolerance is ensured by Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, preserving telemetry in the event of network or service interruptions. The bidirectional integration between the twin and physical assets, governed by observable patterns in the UnrealTwinController, enables continuous verification of state consistency. Extension points in the modular architecture permit the addition of custom analytics or verification services, facilitating ongoing quality assurance and uncertainty quantification throughout the twin lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data is exchanged between the physical buildings and their digital twins through the integration of Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto. The data is streamed to Hono’s ingestion layer and replicated to Ditto, enabling live synchronization of sensor readings and asset state changes. The system is designed as a model‑driven architecture where BIM models are enriched and then deployed as digital twins; data from physical assets is captured by IoT sensors, transmitted to the Hono gateway, and made available to Ditto for real‑time consumption by the DT.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The twin framework supports bidirectional communication; control messages originating from the DT (e.g., HVAC setpoint adjustments or lighting commands) are sent back to the Building Control System (BCS) via the same Eclipse Hono/ Ditto integration, allowing the physical building to be actuated in response to DT decisions. While specific message formats and authentication mechanisms are not detailed in the documents, the architecture guarantees that command flows are routed through the established Hono/ditto channels and that the physical asset receives acknowledgement of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical connection relies on a service‑oriented architecture where Eclipse Hono serves as the central message broker, handling heterogeneous drivers (sensor, actuator, building management). Eclipse Ditto provides an entity registry and event bus over the same transport, enabling real‑time synchronization. The network topology is a multitenant hub‑and‑spoke model with a gateway per building zone, ensuring scalable, low‑latency communication between physical devices and digital twin services.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: the digital twin life‑cycle envisioned by the model‑driven deployment framework consists of four key stages. in the concept‑and‑modeling phase building‑control engineers enrich a BIM model with device semantics, explicitly encoding the spatial layout of sensors, actuators, and controllers as BIM elements. the mde stage applies model‑driven engineering to transform the enriched BIM into structured, machine‑interpretable representations, from which deployable digital‑twin artefacts (including data adapters, command handlers, and persistency stores) are automatically generated. the deployment stage provisions these artefacts onto the target edge or cloud infrastructure, a minimal‑manual, auto‑configuration procedure aligns physical device identifiers with the twin’s runtime entities. the final operation stage real‑time synchronises the physical building and the twin over bidirectional data‑exchange channels, while interactive visualisations expose the twin to facility managers for monitoring and control. governance is enforced through dedicated stakeholder roles—building‑control engineers, facility managers, and dt engineers—each responsible for a lifecycle slice; the framework’s modular architecture and standardised extension points provide maintainable boundaries that facilitate version control and systematic rollback in case of mis‑alignment between bim and runtime models.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: the twinning process combines model‑driven engineering, automated extraction, and modular deployment. building‑control engineers annotate bim models with device semantics and logic. mde transforms enriched bim into structured representations and outputs deployable artefacts. dt engineers integrate custom services and plugins via standardised extension points. the framework’s automated, minimal‑manual configuration ensures that changes to the bim model can be propagated to the running twin with version consistency across artefacts. the process supports evolution by allowing additional custom services to be added and existing ones to be updated without affecting the core mde‑derived model.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: the twin aggregates real‑time sensor streams and integrates them with the enriched bim model, enabling continuous monitoring of building performance. an analytics layer—capable of incorporating ai models or rule‑based inference—derives insights related to cost modelling, lifecycle management, and operational efficiency, which are visualised through interactive dashboards. facility managers can assess these metrics, predict maintenance events, and adjust control logic directly within the twin interface, thereby supporting timely, data‑driven decision making.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the Digital Twin as a system in the AECO domain that merges static BIM representations with real-time data for simulation and optimization. It aligns with the source text's definition of DTs as 'cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation'. It also correctly notes the use of BIM for 'geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics'.

The extraction correctly identifies the services provided, such as 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', and accurately lists the target users with their specific roles. The source text explicitly states: 'DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management... by providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making'. Furthermore, Table I confirms the roles: 'Building Control Engineer... Facility Manager... DT Engineer'.

The extraction accurately details the model-driven framework, the use of MDE, and specific tools like Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine. The source text describes a 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' that 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces'. It also confirms the integration of 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the framework's focus on real-time capabilities and continuous synchronization, as described in the text. It correctly identifies the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for data exchange and the goal of live synchronization. However, it adds interpretive details like 'fine-grained granularity' and 'event-driven' that are not explicitly stated in the provided snippets, preventing a perfect score. Supported by quotes: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization', 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is correct. The source text describes a central hub framework for deploying a Digital Twin and focuses on a Living Lab case study. It does not mention multiple instances, decentralized architectures, or hierarchical structures of Digital Twins.

The description is highly specific and fully supported by the source text. It accurately describes the DT Framework as a 'central hub', mentions the use of 'standardized extension points' for modular integration, and correctly notes the capability for runtime configuration management that avoids full system redeployment. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability by enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment'.

The extraction correctly highlights interoperability as a core non-functional requirement, supported by Section III-A's mention of 'nonfunctional requirements (NFX) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems'. It also notes integration with external systems like BIM models and sensor networks, which aligns with the text's references to 'heterogeneous models' and 'fragmented data landscapes'. However, the absence of specific protocols (e.g., REST, MQTT) is noted in the extraction, but the sources do mention 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' which support such protocols, so the extraction could have been more precise. The score reflects good but not exhaustive alignment.

Fig. 2: Component and connector view of our model-driven DT deployment framework. The DT Framework processes
enriched BIM models and device configurations through validation, conversion, and configuration components. These feed
into middleware (Hono and Ditto) and visualization services (UE Engine), enabling bidirectional integration with the physical

and updates the UE5 scene rendering accordingly. These
programmatic extension points (cf. TwinComponent and
FeatureParser) allow for extending our proposal with additional, custom DT services inside UE5. Observe that
HVACFeatureParser and HVACTwinComponent in Figure 3 are
only for illustrative purposes and currently not implemented in
our proposal. Table IV provides a concise overview on these
extension points.
Our framework ensures data integrity through Eclipse
Ditto’s policy-based validation, which enforces value ranges
and units for sensor data, and pre-execution checks for actuator commands to prevent conflicts or unsafe operations.
Fault tolerance is provided by Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once
message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.

The source mentions sensors and telemetry generally but lacks the technical specifics claimed. data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional

challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
